signal: Cambodia's Funan Techo Canal signals infrastructure-led growth prioritised over trade diversification, deepening Chinese infrastructure dependency

matches.similarity_rationale: Sri Lanka pursued Chinese-financed infrastructure (Hambantota Port as emblematic case) under a dominant-party government with a narrow export and revenue base, creating debt servicing obligations that became fiscally catastrophic when external shocks eroded income. Cambodia's Funan Techo Canal replicates this exact structural configuration: Chinese USD-denominated infrastructure financing, a dominant-party political system with limited fiscal flexibility, and continued dependence on trade preferences (EBA/GSP) as the primary revenue anchor. The governance type, development stage, and external market dependency profiles are the closest match across all cases.
matches.outcome_summary: Sri Lanka defaulted on its sovereign debt in 2022 — the first South Asian country to do so since independence — after infrastructure debt service obligations collided with a collapsed tourism revenue base and ill-timed tax cuts, reducing foreign reserves to near zero and triggering mass protests that ended Rajapaksa's presidency.
matches.cambodia_lesson: Do not allow Chinese infrastructure debt service timelines to be structured on the assumption of uninterrupted trade preference revenue; Cambodia must stress-test Funan Techo Canal debt covenants against a scenario where EBA/GSP revenues fall 20–30% simultaneously, because Sri Lanka's collapse occurred precisely at the intersection of these two vulnerabilities.

matches.similarity_rationale: Bangladesh is the most structurally direct analogue to Cambodia's garment-anchored export base: dominant-party governance with constrained accountability, export concentration above 80% in a single labour-intensive sector, and deep EU/US trade preference dependency as the demand anchor. The Funan Techo Canal signal — prioritising infrastructure FDI attraction over export diversification — mirrors Bangladesh's consistent political choice to deepen RMG capacity rather than use the preference window to build a second export sector, a structural decision that became increasingly costly once the preference margin narrowed. Cambodia's EBA partial withdrawal in 2020 is the exact stress test Bangladesh faces at larger scale.
matches.outcome_summary: Bangladesh sustained 6–7% GDP growth for over two decades on the RMG pillar but failed to develop any second export sector at scale; by 2020 garments still accounted for 84% of exports, and the sector's political power had grown so large that structural transformation became institutionally blocked rather than merely economically difficult.
matches.cambodia_lesson: Infrastructure investment that deepens Chinese dependency without simultaneously forcing export sector diversification will entrench Cambodia in the Bangladesh structural trap — sustained near-term growth that forecloses the medium-term transformation window; Hun Manet must attach explicit second-sector development conditionality to Funan Techo Canal financing timelines or the canal becomes a lock-in mechanism rather than a development catalyst.
